next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

Doug got to the office at ten to eight. He was always early. It wasn't ambition. It was the same instinct that made him check his mirrors twice before changing lanes: on time felt one step from late, and late meant something had gone wrong that he should have prevented. The parking lot was a third full. Roy's car, a 2011 Camry with a dent in the rear quarter panel that Roy had decided years ago was not worth fixing, was already in its usual spot near the loading dock, where nobody else parked because it was farthest from the entrance and because nobody else wanted to walk, which was exactly why Roy parked there.

Mutual Fidelity occupied the second floor of a building on Sawyer Road that also housed a tax preparation service and an orthodontist whose patients could sometimes be heard through the floor, a low hum of suction and distress that Doug had long ago stopped noticing. The elevator was slow enough that Doug took the stairs, which he did not consider exercise. The office was open-plan, thirty desks arranged in rows of five, separated by low partitions that provided the illusion of privacy without any of its function. The carpet was the color of weak tea. The ceiling tiles were acoustic and stained in two places from the building's intermittent plumbing issues, which Doug understood better than the building management did — he'd assessed the office's own water damage claim two years ago, and the repair had not addressed the root cause, which was a compression fitting in the third-floor bathroom that would fail again within eighteen months. He'd noted this...
open_hooks_to_carry: The video hits 14 million by Monday morning and the specific memes and remixes that emerge
Doug at the office — coworkers staring, Janet's deflected conversation, the normalcy of the cubicle now contaminated
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e — but he stopped himself, because the number would be larger now, and larger was a direction he could not yet face.

He washed the glass. He wiped the counter. He stood at the window and watched the neighbor finish scraping the windshield and get in the car and drive away, and the street was empty again, and the quiet of it was the same quiet his apartment always had, except that now the quiet felt like the pause before something, like the space in a claims report between the description of the incident and the estimate of the loss.

The phone rang. He looked at the screen. 310. Los Angeles. Doug had never been to Los Angeles. He did not know anyone in Los Angeles. He had no professional or personal connection to the city, the state, or the time zone, and the number was not one that had any reason to appear on the phone of an insurance adjuster in Columbus, Ohio, on a Sunday afternoon in February.

He didn't answer.

The phone went still. The kitchen was quiet. The heater clicked off and the sudden absence of its noise was louder than the noise had been, and Doug stood there in his sweatpants with crumbs on his shirt and a hangover that had settled into something permanent and low-grade, more a new climate than a temporary condition, and the apartment was exactly what it had been when he'd gone to sleep — the leaning bookshelf, the bread bag twisted shut with its plastic clip, the dish towel on the oven handle — except that somewhere between last night and now, the walls had become thinner, and two million people were on the other side of them.

The phone rang again. 310.
state_deltas_required: The exponential growth of the video's reach and the impossibility of containing its effects on Doug's daily life
Doug's identity becomes public property — the internet constructs a version of him he did not author and cannot correct
